title: The Role of Security Agencies in Curbing Violent Crimes in Nigeria: A Study of Akwa Ibom State
author: Okorie, Ebere James
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: The study investigated the role of security agencies in curbing violent crimes in Akwa Ibom State, Nigeria. Uyo and Itu Local Government Areas were the study area. Descriptive survey research design and historical research design were adopted in the study. The questionnaire was designed in both structured and unstructured formats for the collection of quantitative data. In addition, an In-Depth Interview (IDI) guide was used for the collection of qualitative data among opinion leaders and security personnel. Secondary data were gathered from newspaper and online news. Four hundred (400) respondents were sampled purposively. The population of the study comprises security personnel, opinion and community leaders from the age cohorts of 18 years and above. Out of 400 copies of the questionnaire distributed, only 395 were returned, and the same was used for statistical analysis. Quantitative data collected were statistically presented and analysed using frequency tables and simple percentages. Qualitative data were presented and analysed using the conversational analysis method. Findings revealed that routine patrols, tip-offs, stop and searches, checkpoints/roadblocks, arrests and detentions, and other strategies currently used by security agencies are not enough to deter or reduce the rate of violent crimes in the state. In addition, an ill-equipped workforce, corruption, poor welfare packages, among others, are the major hindrances to effective security architecture in Akwa Ibom State. More so, cultism, aggravated assault, rape, kidnapping, illegal arms, murder, and armed robbery are the most occurring violent crimes in Akwa Ibom State. The study recommends that every community should embrace and adopt community watch as a crime prevention method; cooperation and partnerships between the security agencies and citizens are necessary. There is a need for government at all levels to ensure a permanent place for crime prevention in their structures and programmes; informants who volunteer information or tip-offs on violent crimes to security agencies should be encouraged and protected.
